figures.gloss: Yéil the Raven, who found the world in darkness and stole the sun, moon and stars from the box where the rich chief Nass-shakee-yéil hoarded the light — then loosed them into the sky for all. He brings water and fire to people, gluttonous and cunning in the same breath.
figures.facet: The one figure who is rogue and benefactor inseparably: the world receives the light only as a by-product of theft, greed and a swallowed daughter — civilisation arrives by accident, never by gift.
figures.provenance: Haida/Tlingit Raven (Yéil) cycle; widely attested across NW Coast oral traditions (Swanton, Boas).

figures.gloss: The myth-act in which Raven, born as the chief's grandson, cries for the bentwood boxes until he is given the stars, then the moon, then the sun — and escaping as a bird, releases each into the heavens, ordering the cosmos by liberation rather than fabrication.
figures.facet: Here the sun is not begotten nor forged but stolen and released: light is property redistributed, the firmament made by a heist.
figures.provenance: The 'Box of Daylight' episode, central to the Raven cycle.

figures.gloss: The remote elder chief who, in the time before light, kept the sun, moon and stars sealed in nested boxes at the head of the Nass River — the dim originating power from whom the lit world must be taken, not received.
figures.facet: A creator-figure who hoards rather than gives: the deus otiosus inverted into a miser, so that the world's beginning is a dispossession of the maker, not his benefaction.
figures.provenance: Tlingit; the name and role vary across tellings — sometimes conflated with a separate Raven, an instability the tradition itself preserves.

figures.gloss: The great Chief of the Undersea World (Haida Konankada, Tlingit Gonaqadét), lord of a house beneath the ocean whose doorway is a face; master of the wealth, salmon and fortune that rise from the deep to the living.
figures.facet: The sea-sovereign is a house-holding chief, not a formless deep: the ocean is read as a noble lineage's longhouse, the underworld modelled on the clan hall.
figures.provenance: Haida/Tlingit sea-being; Gonaqadét associated with sudden wealth and danger.

figures.gloss: The vast bird whose wingbeats are thunder and whose blinking eyes throw lightning; it preys on the whale, lifting it from the sea, and rules the upper air as the storm made flesh.
figures.facet: Thunder is not a god's hammer but a living predator — the storm imagined as an animal that hunts, locked in eternal combat with the whale of the deep.
figures.provenance: Pan-NW-Coast; central to Kwakwaka'wakw and Nuu-chah-nulth as well, present in Haida/Tlingit crest art.

figures.gloss: The woman Raven married who, washing her hands in the bilge, brought forth the first salmon and filled the smokehouses — and when slighted, walked back into the sea taking every fish with her, so the runs follow her still.
figures.facet: The salmon-mother whose generosity is conditional: the staple of life is a marriage-gift that can be withdrawn, binding subsistence to the keeping of respect.
figures.provenance: Tlingit Fog Woman (Kaxdakw / Creek Woman) narrative.

figures.gloss: The great ceremonial of giving and destroying wealth by which rank, names and crests are validated and the cosmic ledger of obligation is kept; status is real only when given away, and the world is held in order by the binding force of reciprocity.
figures.facet: Right-order is enacted as economy: the law the cosmos obeys is the law of the gift — to receive is to owe, and balance is maintained by deliberate dispossession.
figures.provenance: Potlatch as institution (Haida/Tlingit and pan-NW Coast); read here as the tradition's cosmic-order principle.

figures.gloss: Society divided into two reciprocal moieties — Raven and Eagle (Wolf among the Tlingit) — each holding inherited crests, names and stories; one may marry and bury only across the line, so the whole people is a single body whose two halves complete each other.
figures.facet: Duality here is not metaphysics but kinship law made paired: the two are not warring forces but the obligatory partners who must intermarry for the world to continue.
figures.provenance: Matrilineal moiety system, Haida and Tlingit.

figures.gloss: Shape-shifting otter-people of Tlingit and Haida belief who lure the drowned, the lost, and the freezing to their doom, transforming the rescued-turned-captive into one of their own kind before they can be recovered by kin.
figures.facet: The most feared beings in the cosmology are also its deepest reservoir of power: mastered by a shaman, the same land-otter tongues that steal souls become his strongest yék helper-spirits — danger and power drawn from the identical source.
figures.provenance: Frederica de Laguna, Under Mount Saint Elias (1972); John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909).

figures.gloss: A high-born woman who insults bear droppings on a berrying trail, is taken by a bear prince as his wife, and bears half-human, half-bear sons; her eventual return with her children to her human village founds the lineages entitled to wear the bear crest.
figures.facet: The charter narrative behind an entire category of crest-property: descent from an animal spouse is the mechanism by which a specific clan, not humankind in general, gains a specific inherited right.
figures.provenance: Haida/Tsimshian narrative cycle; Marius Barbeau, Bear Mother (1946); recorded widely in Haida and Tsimshian oral literature.

figures.gloss: A Tlingit boy who mocks a dried salmon is swept away and taken in by the Salmon People, living a full cycle among them upriver and back to sea before returning to his village as a shaman who teaches the rites of respect owed to the fish.
figures.facet: The charter myth of the salmon covenant told from the human side: disrespect costs a person his humanity temporarily, and only a completed death-and-return journey earns the authority to teach the rules that keep the runs coming back.
figures.provenance: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909); widely attested across NW Coast oral tradition.

figures.gloss: A Haida sea-monster combining wolf and killer-whale features, whose skin a hero dons to hunt whales by night, bringing home unearned wealth of meat and oil to his village each morning.
figures.facet: A crest-monster distinct from, yet entangled with, the Undersea Chief's domain: wealth from the deep here comes not as a gift bestowed by a sovereign but as prey taken by a human wearing a stolen predator's skin.
figures.provenance: John R. Swanton, Haida Texts and Myths (1905).

figures.gloss: The Tlingit religious specialist who fasts alone in the wilderness to bind yék helper-spirits — often the tongues of land otters — to himself, then uses them to diagnose and cure illness, counter witchcraft, and treat with the spirit world on the village's behalf.
figures.facet: Regalia and body were held to remain charged with power after death: his mask, rattles, and grave-house were left untouched, feared and respected rather than inherited like ordinary property.
figures.provenance: Frederica de Laguna, Under Mount Saint Elias (1972).

figures.gloss: A Haida being encountered in the forest whose sight, or the cry of the infant she carries, confers sudden wealth and good fortune on whoever perceives her — a female personification of luck as a form of property that can be won.
figures.facet: Fortune here is not earned through exchange but granted by a chance encounter with a numinous woman, a wealth-cosmology distinct from, and prior to, the potlatch's economy of deliberate reciprocal giving.
figures.provenance: John R. Swanton, Haida Texts and Myths (1905).

figures.gloss: The documented Tlingit/Haida belief that a person's hair, nail-parings, spittle, or worn and cast-off clothing carry a portion of that person's vulnerability, such that a sorcerer who obtains these leavings can work harm on the victim from a distance.
figures.facet: The corrected referent for this roster's soul/object-animism gap: personhood is here treated as physically distributed into what a body sheds, not as a unitary interior soul, and it is this distributed vulnerability — not the potlatch's economy of rank — that hostile magic exploits.
figures.provenance: Sergei Kan, Symbolic Immortality: The Tlingit Potlatch of the Nineteenth Century (1989); Frederica de Laguna, Under Mount Saint Elias (1972).

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The manifestation of supreme divine power as a terrifying, earth-shaking acoustic phenomenon.

Two North-Pacific lords of the sea who send the deep's wealth and game to coastal villages; the undersea-chief of the Haida/Tlingit and the orca-kamuy of the Ainu rhyme as bestowers of marine abundance, parallel and unconnected.

Both encode the 'return-the-game' covenant: as the salmon follow Fog Woman back to the sea when slighted, the bear-kamuy comes again only if sent home with proper respect — subsistence held by the keeping of courtesy to the animal-giver.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Greedy, bungling figures whose selfish actions accidentally reshape the human condition, often introducing hardship.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions utilize strict genealogical mapping as the ultimate claim to social, spiritual, and territorial inheritance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The divine, winged emissary who crosses the celestial boundary to bring critical survival technology to a suffering early humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are apex mythic predators (one marine, one aerial) deeply associated with the hunting and control of whales for coastal peoples.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A wealthy, distant chief of the horizon who hoards massive resources and must be spiritually negotiated with.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Extensive folkloric and ethnographic evidence links the Paleo-Siberian and Pacific Northwest Raven cycles as a shared Beringian cultural inheritance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The overarching Raven figure who brings light and structure to a dark, fluid world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The powerful female counterpart to the male creator/trickster, wielding magic intimately tied to sustenance and nature.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The naive but powerful initiator who, by stepping into the void or breaking the rules, sets the entire structured cycle of creation into motion.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both view the human essence as geographically extended and vulnerable; capturing a footprint or a cast-off item allows a sorcerer remote access to the victim's nervous system.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both operate on the animistic belief that material items possess a spirit that must be released through ritual destruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions conceive of a divisible soul that can detach, wander, or be intentionally stolen by sorcery, necessitating shamanic retrieval.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The creator who operates through theft, trickery, and subversion rather than omnipotent fiat.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Extreme social stratification achieved entirely through the formalized, competitive display and ritual destruction of vast material wealth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme trickster who shapes human destiny through deceit, magic, and relentless boundary-crossing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shared archetype of an apex avian predator serving as the fierce champion of the Upper World against deep-water subterranean monsters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are animal tricksters who rely on deceit rather than physical strength to steal foundational resources—such as fire or light—for humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The colossal avian entity whose physical movements mechanically generate extreme weather.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Pacific Northwest crests are strictly inherited corporate property signifying aristocratic lineage, whereas neo-shamanic power animals are radically democratic, individualized psychological allies.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shared archetype of giant avian sky-beings whose physical movements directly generate the meteorological phenomena of thunder and lightning.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A monstrous, chimerical earth/sea being whose body forms the foundation of the world and generates natural wealth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An apex celestial raptor whose iconography is leveraged by human elites to signify divine violence and atmospheric power.
Theft of light/fire from a hoarding power for the benefit of humankind — the benefactor-by-larceny pattern. No descent or contact; a structural rhyme across unrelated families.
Both are trickster culture-heroes who master the sun — Maui snares and slows it, Raven steals and releases it. Parallel of the trickster bending the solar order to human use.
Trickster as indispensable mediator: Esu the required crossroads-gate, Raven the agent who alone moves between sealed cosmic boxes and the lit world.
Trickster whose appetite and deceit nonetheless drive cosmic events; the rogue inside the order rather than outside it.
Both are dark, shape-shifting trickster-makers entangled with the origin of the lit world; an interpretive reach, the resemblance loose.
The defining act of the Raven cycle — the figure and his signature deed.
Where Ra is the self-generating solar source the cosmos issues from, here the sun is pre-existing property stolen and released — light by redistribution, not emanation.
Both turn on the sun's confinement and release — Amaterasu lured from the cave, the sun loosed from the box; the lit world restored by drawing light out of enclosure.
Within the tradition: the hoarding elder against the liberating grandson — creation framed as the maker dispossessed by the trickster.
A remote sky-elder at the origin, but inverted into a miser — a withdrawn-creator read against the Indo-European luminous sky-father. Speculative, flagged.
Sovereign of a realm below — the undersea house as analogue to the underworld throne, master of what the living cannot reach.
Both personify a vast elemental domain as the source of sustenance, though one is the sea-chief and one the earth-mother; a loose mother-of-waters/great-source rhyme.
The storm-power as a sky-warrior — but Thunderbird is the storm as living predator, not a god wielding a weapon. Functional storm-wielder parallel, divergent in form.
Thunder personified as a sky-being whose body makes the storm; both render thunder as embodied rather than instrumental.
Storm-power locked in combat with a great water-beast (Thunderbird/whale ~ Indra/Vrtra) — the resemblance is real but distant; flagged.
The provider-mother whose withdrawal takes the staple food with her — salmon-runs as Fog Woman's gift, grain as Demeter's; subsistence bound to a goddess's keeping.
Within the cycle: Raven's wife whose slighting drives the salmon back to sea — the trickster's appetite costing the world its abundance.
Both name an impersonal order the world is held in — but the potlatch's law is the law of the gift and reciprocity, not the Dao's effortless way. A reach across very different registers.
An immanent ordering force enacted through ritual rather than a personal deity; loose structural kinship, flagged.
Reality organised as two complementary halves that require each other — but here it is kinship law (Raven/Eagle moieties must intermarry), not metaphysical polarity.
Within the tradition: the moiety system and the potlatch are one machine — crests and rank are validated only by reciprocal giving across the two halves.
The moiety embodies complementarity as social fact: the whole people is one body only because its two halves are bound to complete each other.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems equate massive, performative wealth distribution and intentional debt creation with spiritual authority and cosmic equilibrium.

Loose: both make competitive, public giving a mechanism of religious status and communal finance — but the Pacific Northwest potlatch redistributes rank through destruction and largesse, where nsinsani is congregational fundraising by comparison. Shared shape of 'giving as public contest,' different stakes and machinery. Flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two North American kinship systems, unrelated by descent, that both root clan identity and social obligation in inherited animal-ancestor crests.
Intra-tradition: the Land Otter Men are the shaman's chief adversary until bound, at which point their tongues become his yék helper-spirits — the same beings as threat and as power source.

Intra-tradition: two halves of the same salmon-respect covenant — Fog Woman is the source who can withdraw the run, Salmon Boy the human who is taken into it and returns to teach the rites that keep her from doing so.
Intra-tradition: Wasgo's night-hunting and undersea wealth entangle it with the Gonaqadét/Undersea-Chief complex, though Haida narrative keeps it a distinct crest figure with its own cycle.

Cross-tradition: a woman's union with a bear founds a human lineage — the Haida/Tsimshian Bear Mother's half-bear sons and the Korean Bear-Woman's founding son, structurally parallel animal-ancestress narratives with no claimed contact.

Cross-tradition: both belong to the circumpolar bear-ceremonialism belt documented across the Northern Hemisphere (Hallowell, 1926) — kinship-with-the-bear narrative and bear-sending rite as regional variants of one widespread complex.

Cross-tradition: unrelated circumpolar shamanic specialists whose regalia is treated as independently charged with power and whose office is to travel to, or treat with, the spirit world for the community.
Cross-tradition: both are shamans specialized toward the darker, witching and curing end of the craft rather than its benevolent pole — structural parallel, no claimed contact.
Cross-tradition: both view the human essence as geographically extended and vulnerable through cast-off traces — a footprint or personal leaving giving a sorcerer remote access to the victim.
Cross-tradition: both hold that part of the self can detach into an object or trace and be seized by sorcery from a distance, requiring specialist countermeasures.

Both are birds whose action literally constitutes thunder or lightning rather than merely causing it — but the Pacific Northwest Thunderbird is a revered nature-spirit, while Impundulu is characteristically a witch's owned familiar.

dossier.essence: The traditional religions of the Haida (Haida Gwaii) and Tlingit (the southeast Alaska panhandle and adjacent coast), matrilineal, rank-conscious peoples of the North Pacific Coast whose societies divide into reciprocal moieties (Raven and Eagle among the Haida; Raven and Wolf among the Tlingit). These are living traditions of an animate world in which humans, animals, and spirit-beings are all 'people,' and in which the trickster-transformer Raven (Yéil) set the present order.
dossier.cosmos: The world is thick with non-human persons — salmon, bear, and killer-whale peoples with their own villages beneath sea and forest, and a sea-chief (Haida Konankada, Tlingit Gonaqadét) whose house sends up wealth. Raven did not fabricate the cosmos but ordered it by theft and trickery, releasing the sun, moon, and stars from the nested boxes of a hoarding elder. Souls are widely held to be reborn within their own matriline, so the dead return among their descendants.
dossier.practice: The pivotal institution is the potlatch — a host lineage's feast of oratory, dance, and the giving away or destruction of wealth to validate names, crests, and rank and, above all, to complete the obligations of a death (the memorial potlatch). Crest art (poles, house-fronts, regalia), initiation into dancing societies, and the work of shamans (Tlingit íxt') who healed and controlled spirits and weather round out ritual life; moiety exogamy governs marriage and mutual obligation.
dossier.canon: Oral — clan histories, crest narratives, and the Raven cycle, transmitted by trained narrators and recorded in the early 20th century by John R. Swanton and others, but remaining a living, owned oral literature rather than a scripture.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds a cosmos governed by the law of the gift — rank, wealth, and cosmic balance made real only by giving away, so that religion and economy are one ledger of reciprocal obligation.
sources: John R. Swanton, 'Tlingit Myths and Texts' (1909)
Frederica de Laguna, 'Under Mount Saint Elias: The History and Culture of the Yakutat Tlingit' (1972)
Sergei Kan, 'Symbolic Immortality: The Tlingit Potlatch of the Nineteenth Century' (1989)
Robert Bringhurst, 'A Story as Sharp as a Knife: The Classical Haida Mythtellers' (1999)
